title: Samsung S26 Ultra - Best Camera Settings for Video and Photos!
description:
This is a practical, in-depth tutorial for the Samsung Galaxy S26 Ultra powered by the Snapdragon 8 Elite Gen 5 Mobile Platform for Galaxy.
We'll start with the essential photo settings and composition tools, then move into more advanced controls like autofocus tracking, resolution choices, and how the Qualcomm Spectra ISP + Neural Processing Unit work together for real-time scene optimization.
You’ll also see how the new Virtual Reflector works, which framerate to choose for video, and a full breakdown of APV LOG, including how it differs from HEVC and when to use each format.

Gear I Used in This video:
Freewell x StevenDivish Anamorphic Lens - http://bit.ly/4hgBHu7

Finally, I show two workflows for editing APV LOG footage: one directly on the S26 Ultra, and one on a computer for full professional control.

title: Saving a Broken Vintage Mirrorless: Lumix G2
description: Got this old Panasonic Lumix G2 with a totally black screen. At first it looked like the LCD was gone, but after opening it up I found the real problem — a torn flex cable inside the flip screen hinge. I swapped the whole swivel screen mount with another one that had a good flex cable. Put everything back together, powered it on, and the screen came right back to life.

title: Amazon Budget Pistol Setup You’ll Actually Use (And What to Skip for Your EDC)
description:
If you carry to protect your family, you probably think about providing for them too. That's what The Modern Provider Podcast is about, check it out here: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=y5WG1ojh4jQ

Want the full breakdown of how I use YouTube to build real income around our family? Watch my free masterclass here: https://dougjohnsonofficial.com/free-training

Get the Osight SE on Sale Now (thanks Osight for sponsoring the video!)
Purchase here:
Osight Store: https://www.osight.com/s/MBXO49
Amazon: https://amzn.to/4vDuYRr
Sale Period: April 17th, 10:00 AM EDT – April 24th, 11:59 PM EDT

Amazon Gear I Recommend in the Video
Osight SE Green: https://amzn.to/4vDuYRr
Alien Gear Photon: https://amzn.to/4mENViO
Streamlight TLR-7 HL-X: https://amzn.to/4mENViO
Glock 19 Magwell: https://amzn.to/4tTtWiu
Feyachi Light: https://amzn.to/42cilzo
Snap Caps: https://amzn.to/4mDJgNP

Brothers: Not everybody has the budget for top-tier concealed carry gear, and not everybody needs the most expensive setup on the market. But at the same time, Amazon is full of absolute garbage when it comes to optics, holsters, lights, and other gun-related accessories. So in this video, I put together a budget Amazon CCW / EDC setup to see what’s actually worth buying, what’s surprisingly decent, and what should probably stay in the cart forever.

I tested some of the highest-rated and cheapest options I could find across the three essentials: pistol lights, holsters, and optics. Some of it was exactly as sketchy as expected, some of it was better than I thought, and a few products actually felt like something a normal person could realistically use without spending a fortune. I also compared a few of these budget options against the gear I normally carry so there’s a real point of reference instead of just random Amazon gambling.

If you’ve ever wondered whether there’s such a thing as budget CCW gear that’s actually usable, this video is for you. Drop a comment with your favorite hidden Amazon gun gear finds, let me know what I missed, and if this video does well maybe I’ll do a round two with even more weird budget gear. Big thanks to Osight for sponsoring part of this video and sending over the Osight SE for comparison.

title: Sony A7C II Review – Is This the Perfect Everyday Camera?
description:
Sony A7C II Review – The Perfect Everyday Camera?

In this video, I take a deep dive into the Sony A7C II and share my real-world experience using it as a daily carry camera. I’ve been seriously impressed with how compact and lightweight it is without sacrificing performance—making it a great option for anyone who wants full-frame quality in a smaller setup.

One feature I’ve really enjoyed is the 2.35:1 aspect marker setting, which gives photos a cinematic, movie-like feel right in-camera. It’s a small detail, but it adds a lot creatively if you like that widescreen look.

That said, no camera is perfect. I also go over some of the downsides and limitations I’ve noticed after using it, so you can decide if it’s the right fit for you.

Brothers: Not everybody has the budget for top-tier concealed carry gear, and not everybody needs the most expensive setup on the market. But at the same time, Amazon is full of absolute garbage when it comes to optics, holsters, lights, and other gun-related accessories. So in this video, I put together a budget Amazon CCW / EDC setup to see what’s actually worth buying, what’s surprisingly decent, and what should probably stay in the cart forever.

I tested some of the highest-rated and cheapest options I could find across the three essentials: pistol lights, holsters, and optics. Some of it was exactly as sketchy as expected, some of it was better than I thought, and a few products actually felt like something a normal person could realistically use without spending a fortune. I also compared a few of these budget options against the gear I normally carry so there’s a real point of reference instead of just random Amazon gambling.

If you’ve ever wondered whether there’s such a thing as budget CCW gear that’s actually usable, this video is for you. Drop a comment with your favorite hidden Amazon gun gear finds, let me know what I missed, and if this video does well maybe I’ll do a round two with even more weird budget gear. Big thanks to Osight for sponsoring part of this video and sending over the Osight SE for comparison.

title: Saving a Broken Vintage Mirrorless: Lumix G2
description: Got this old Panasonic Lumix G2 with a totally black screen. At first it looked like the LCD was gone, but after opening it up I found the real problem — a torn flex cable inside the flip screen hinge. I swapped the whole swivel screen mount with another one that had a good flex cable. Put everything back together, powered it on, and the screen came right back to life.

text_original:
@dougjohnsonofficial I'll start by saying I've met people that said they didn't have any issues with their Gen 3, but my buddy and I both did.  He sold his.  We both used a variety of ammo. Blazer Brass, Fiocchi, PMC...115gr and 124gr.  I wanted it to work so badly that after my first 2 day class with a bunch of malfunctions I spent the $100 on the Radian guide rod kit.  I emailed Radian and they said to use hot ammo and break it in.  So far, with only 300 rounds through it with the Radian guid rod, still getting the occasional malfunction.  So it's a range toy/training aid until we figure it out.  CCW and USPSA will have to wait.  
Let me know how it goes for you. Us Cali shooters have to work with what we got.  Like my stupid Advantage Arms .22lr conversion kit sucks, but I'm not spending $1,000 on the G44.  haha

In this video, I take a deep dive into the Sony A7C II and share my real-world experience using it as a daily carry camera. title: Sony A7C II Review – Is This the Perfect Everyday Camera?
description:
Sony A7C II Review – The Perfect Everyday Camera?

In this video, I take a deep dive into the Sony A7C II and share my real-world experience using it as a daily carry camera. I’ve been seriously impressed with how compact and lightweight it is without sacrificing performance—making it a great option for anyone who wants full-frame quality in a smaller setup.

One feature I’ve really enjoyed is the 2.35:1 aspect marker setting, which gives photos a cinematic, movie-like feel right in-camera. It’s a small detail, but it adds a lot creatively if you like that widescreen look.

That said, no camera is perfect. I also go over some of the downsides and limitations I’ve noticed after using it, so you can decide if it’s the right fit for you.

If you’re considering the A7C II or looking for a versatile everyday camera, this review should help you out.

Let me know your thoughts in the comments, and don’t forget to like and subscribe for more gear reviews!

description:
In this video, I test the K&F Concept Alpha 10L camera sling to see if downsizing your setup actually improves your travel photography experience. Instead of packing everything “just in case”, I limit myself to what fits inside this small sling and put it through a full day of shooting.

I break down what you can realistically fit inside, from simple camera setups to drone and vlogging options, and show the trade-offs you’ll have to make along the way. More importantly, I take it out into real travel conditions to see how it actually performs when you’re walking, shooting, and dealing with the heat.

If you’re tired of overpacking and want a lighter, simpler setup for travel photography, this might change how you think about your gear.

Brothers: Not everybody has the budget for top-tier concealed carry gear, and not everybody needs the most expensive setup on the market. But at the same time, Amazon is full of absolute garbage when it comes to optics, holsters, lights, and other gun-related accessories. So in this video, I put together a budget Amazon CCW / EDC setup to see what’s actually worth buying, what’s surprisingly decent, and what should probably stay in the cart forever.

I tested some of the highest-rated and cheapest options I could find across the three essentials: pistol lights, holsters, and optics. Some of it was exactly as sketchy as expected, some of it was better than I thought, and a few products actually felt like something a normal person could realistically use without spending a fortune. I also compared a few of these budget options against the gear I normally carry so there’s a real point of reference instead of just random Amazon gambling.

If you’ve ever wondered whether there’s such a thing as budget CCW gear that’s actually usable, this video is for you. Drop a comment with your favorite hidden Amazon gun gear finds, let me know what I missed, and if this video does well maybe I’ll do a round two with even more weird budget gear. Big thanks to Osight for sponsoring part of this video and sending over the Osight SE for comparison.

title: Samsung S26 Ultra - Best Camera Settings for Video and Photos!
description:
This is a practical, in-depth tutorial for the Samsung Galaxy S26 Ultra powered by the Snapdragon 8 Elite Gen 5 Mobile Platform for Galaxy.
We'll start with the essential photo settings and composition tools, then move into more advanced controls like autofocus tracking, resolution choices, and how the Qualcomm Spectra ISP + Neural Processing Unit work together for real-time scene optimization.
You’ll also see how the new Virtual Reflector works, which framerate to choose for video, and a full breakdown of APV LOG, including how it differs from HEVC and when to use each format.

Brothers: Not everybody has the budget for top-tier concealed carry gear, and not everybody needs the most expensive setup on the market. But at the same time, Amazon is full of absolute garbage when it comes to optics, holsters, lights, and other gun-related accessories. So in this video, I put together a budget Amazon CCW / EDC setup to see what’s actually worth buying, what’s surprisingly decent, and what should probably stay in the cart forever.

I tested some of the highest-rated and cheapest options I could find across the three essentials: pistol lights, holsters, and optics. Some of it was exactly as sketchy as expected, some of it was better than I thought, and a few products actually felt like something a normal person could realistically use without spending a fortune. I also compared a few of these budget options against the gear I normally carry so there’s a real point of reference instead of just random Amazon gambling.

If you’ve ever wondered whether there’s such a thing as budget CCW gear that’s actually usable, this video is for you. Drop a comment with your favorite hidden Amazon gun gear finds, let me know what I missed, and if this video does well maybe I’ll do a round two with even more weird budget gear. text_original: This videos so corny bro. Too many jokes about corn and not enough about peas. I love corn as much as the next guy, but peas are just superior when done right. Peas need justice. Why focus on corn when you could focus on peas, i mean theyre equally as good and taste better. Peas could go with really anything. But i guess corn could too. I guess the point im trying to get here is maybe peas, corn, and green beans should all come together as the best foods. Right? Or maybe im just delusional, and peas are actually the best food, corn is too messy and green beans are too bitter, thats why i think peas are better. But thats just my opinion, maybe someone else loves the bitterness of green beans or the cleanup after eating corn. But if its dry corn? Thats a whole different story. Who monsters eat dry corn? Really? You really couldnt have chosen something else other than dry corn? Why not peas, dry or not they still taste good. Thats the versatility of peas i guess. Peas are superior. I was lowkey right.

Want the full breakdown of how I use YouTube to build real income around our family? Watch my free masterclass here: https://dougjohnsonofficial.com/free-training

Osight SE Green: https://amzn.to/4vDuYRr

Streamlight TLR-7 HL-X: https://amzn.to/4mENViO
Glock 19 Magwell: https://amzn.to/4tTtWiu
Feyachi Light: https://amzn.to/42cilzo
Snap Caps: https://amzn.to/4mDJgNP

Brothers: Not everybody has the budget for top-tier concealed carry gear, and not everybody needs the most expensive setup on the market. But at the same time, Amazon is full of absolute garbage when it comes to optics, holsters, lights, and other gun-related accessories. So in this video, I put together a budget Amazon CCW / EDC setup to see what’s actually worth buying, what’s surprisingly decent, and what should probably stay in the cart forever.

I tested some of the highest-rated and cheapest options I could find across the three essentials: pistol lights, holsters, and optics. Some of it was exactly as sketchy as expected, some of it was better than I thought, and a few products actually felt like something a normal person could realistically use without spending a fortune. I also compared a few of these budget options against the gear I normally carry so there’s a real point of reference instead of just random Amazon gambling.

If you’ve ever wondered whether there’s such a thing as budget CCW gear that’s actually usable, this video is for you. Drop a comment with your favorite hidden Amazon gun gear finds, let me know what I missed, and if this video does well maybe I’ll do a round two with even more weird budget gear. Big thanks to Osight for sponsoring part of this video and sending over the Osight SE for comparison.

text_original:
I really appreciate you sharing your travel insights and taking the time to watch while you ate! 👍🏾

Let’s me get to answering your questions. 

I did not get to use a public drinking stations there we usually filled up our water at our hotel and then got things to drink when we would get food.

I haven’t seen one that attaches in a more permanent way for action cameras. I know that they have them for regular cameras though. Is there a brand or product you can let me know about?

Yeah, I don’t think 25,000mAh is the right fit for me with the way that I travel. I like the idea of having something separate from my phone and the one that I showed in the video is super slim so I feel being able to use your phone and have that on it. It won’t add too much weight and I don’t have to plug it in.

I definitely like a sling but not what I’m carrying so many things I think if I had one camera instead of two, it would’ve been OK if I didn’t want to carry a water bottleI did only use the iPads on the plane and play video games in the room at the end of days. I didn’t need to use a photo dump because I took a 256 gig memory card for my Q3 and a 256 card for my Action Camera as well. 

I hope that answers everything if you have any other questions, let me know, though, happy to help

Brothers: Not everybody has the budget for top-tier concealed carry gear, and not everybody needs the most expensive setup on the market. But at the same time, Amazon is full of absolute garbage when it comes to optics, holsters, lights, and other gun-related accessories. So in this video, I put together a budget Amazon CCW / EDC setup to see what’s actually worth buying, what’s surprisingly decent, and what should probably stay in the cart forever.

I tested some of the highest-rated and cheapest options I could find across the three essentials: pistol lights, holsters, and optics. Some of it was exactly as sketchy as expected, some of it was better than I thought, and a few products actually felt like something a normal person could realistically use without spending a fortune. I also compared a few of these budget options against the gear I normally carry so there’s a real point of reference instead of just random Amazon gambling.

If you’ve ever wondered whether there’s such a thing as budget CCW gear that’s actually usable, this video is for you. Drop a comment with your favorite hidden Amazon gun gear finds, let me know what I missed, and if this video does well maybe I’ll do a round two with even more weird budget gear. text_original:
I'm glad your video popped up in my feed again. 
-One thing I was curious about because I watched a video years ago about the public drinking stations in Paris. Did you take advantage of that, is that still a thing? 
-I see you have the clip, have you thought about the one that gets attached a bit more permanent.  
-On the big battery, i've had my reservations and am glad that i've held off on getting one. My wife and I just take a 10k mah battery pack each with us and seems to do the job. 
-I'd say give the sling bag a try again, yes you can keep essentials in your backpack, but I love keeping a all of our documents, phone, extra cash close to my chest. There are alot of pro's especially for travel. 
-You showed the ipad and said you used it for flights only, did you ever need to do a data dump from your cameras somewhere? 

Thanks for the video, watched it while eating my lunch 😅

text_original: Lot of the cheaper lights have side buttons, which isnt practical or work well in a holster.   I got a Vtiger PL1 1000 lumens, that has rear facing controls and tucks away nicely on my Glock19.   500 + rounds using it and it is staying strong.  Will set you back $25.   The Gencom ones ive tried were not worth it.    UUQ has a mini reflex green dot for around $60, that I got for my Mark IV and has been great.  $52 atm and is very compact in size.   I have the Osight C, which has been fantastic and was around $189,  it keeps up with my holosun(which have come down in price lately and are often under $200).   I do have a couple of CV life optics, a reflex on my BB gun, and a Fake Acog on my AR, that even after 1000 rounds still holds zero and works well.  Would it hold up to being banged around in the field, probably not, but its fine for a range gun.

Feyachi Light: https://amzn.to/42cilzo
Snap Caps: https://amzn.to/4mDJgNP

Brothers: Not everybody has the budget for top-tier concealed carry gear, and not everybody needs the most expensive setup on the market. But at the same time, Amazon is full of absolute garbage when it comes to optics, holsters, lights, and other gun-related accessories. So in this video, I put together a budget Amazon CCW / EDC setup to see what’s actually worth buying, what’s surprisingly decent, and what should probably stay in the cart forever.

I tested some of the highest-rated and cheapest options I could find across the three essentials: pistol lights, holsters, and optics. Some of it was exactly as sketchy as expected, some of it was better than I thought, and a few products actually felt like something a normal person could realistically use without spending a fortune. I also compared a few of these budget options against the gear I normally carry so there’s a real point of reference instead of just random Amazon gambling.

If you’ve ever wondered whether there’s such a thing as budget CCW gear that’s actually usable, this video is for you. Drop a comment with your favorite hidden Amazon gun gear finds, let me know what I missed, and if this video does well maybe I’ll do a round two with even more weird budget gear. Big thanks to Osight for sponsoring part of this video and sending over the Osight SE for comparison.

Brothers: Not everybody has the budget for top-tier concealed carry gear, and not everybody needs the most expensive setup on the market. But at the same time, Amazon is full of absolute garbage when it comes to optics, holsters, lights, and other gun-related accessories. So in this video, I put together a budget Amazon CCW / EDC setup to see what’s actually worth buying, what’s surprisingly decent, and what should probably stay in the cart forever.

I tested some of the highest-rated and cheapest options I could find across the three essentials: pistol lights, holsters, and optics. Some of it was exactly as sketchy as expected, some of it was better than I thought, and a few products actually felt like something a normal person could realistically use without spending a fortune. I also compared a few of these budget options against the gear I normally carry so there’s a real point of reference instead of just random Amazon gambling.

If you’ve ever wondered whether there’s such a thing as budget CCW gear that’s actually usable, this video is for you. Drop a comment with your favorite hidden Amazon gun gear finds, let me know what I missed, and if this video does well maybe I’ll do a round two with even more weird budget gear. text_original: The TXC CVRT is extremely underrated. I’ve been carrying since 2012 and probably owned 30-40 holsters. I’ve used Tier 1, Tenicor, T-Rex, and lots of others. The TXC CVRT is my absolute favorite currently. It feels like a high performance holster. They basically designed if for USPSA shooters who want to compete with appendix rigs, but without sacrificing concealment. It’s very similar to the Tenicor Velo5 (my second favorite) but I like the TXC a little better.

Glock 19 Magwell: https://amzn.to/4tTtWiu
Feyachi Light: https://amzn.to/42cilzo
Snap Caps: https://amzn.to/4mDJgNP

Brothers: Not everybody has the budget for top-tier concealed carry gear, and not everybody needs the most expensive setup on the market. But at the same time, Amazon is full of absolute garbage when it comes to optics, holsters, lights, and other gun-related accessories. So in this video, I put together a budget Amazon CCW / EDC setup to see what’s actually worth buying, what’s surprisingly decent, and what should probably stay in the cart forever.

I tested some of the highest-rated and cheapest options I could find across the three essentials: pistol lights, holsters, and optics. Some of it was exactly as sketchy as expected, some of it was better than I thought, and a few products actually felt like something a normal person could realistically use without spending a fortune. I also compared a few of these budget options against the gear I normally carry so there’s a real point of reference instead of just random Amazon gambling.

If you’ve ever wondered whether there’s such a thing as budget CCW gear that’s actually usable, this video is for you. Drop a comment with your favorite hidden Amazon gun gear finds, let me know what I missed, and if this video does well maybe I’ll do a round two with even more weird budget gear. Big thanks to Osight for sponsoring part of this video and sending over the Osight SE for comparison.

description:
I added a simple but important upgrade to my Fujifilm X100VI.
Adapter ring + protection filter gives extra safety and basic weather resistance.
Perfect setup for everyday carry and street shooting.

text_original: 8:14 I've purchased holsters from QVO tactical, legacy firearms, and Vedder holsters just to name a few so I decided to try a few budget options. I have the Deerco and it is VERY bulky, it's a cheap copy of the Safari and Incog X. I really like the FDO Industries +1 Flex. I did end up swapping the clips out to DCC clips for more adjustments. A big plus is the Glock 19 lightbearing model also fits my CZ P-10c with some adjustment to the retention screws.

I tested some of the highest-rated and cheapest options I could find across the three essentials: pistol lights, holsters, and optics. I also compared a few of these budget options against the gear I normally carry so there’s a real point of reference instead of just random Amazon gambling.

If you’ve ever wondered whether there’s such a thing as budget CCW gear that’s actually usable, this video is for you. Brothers: Not everybody has the budget for top-tier concealed carry gear, and not everybody needs the most expensive setup on the market. But at the same time, Amazon is full of absolute garbage when it comes to optics, holsters, lights, and other gun-related accessories. So in this video, I put together a budget Amazon CCW / EDC setup to see what’s actually worth buying, what’s surprisingly decent, and what should probably stay in the cart forever.

I tested some of the highest-rated and cheapest options I could find across the three essentials: pistol lights, holsters, and optics. Some of it was exactly as sketchy as expected, some of it was better than I thought, and a few products actually felt like something a normal person could realistically use without spending a fortune. I also compared a few of these budget options against the gear I normally carry so there’s a real point of reference instead of just random Amazon gambling.

If you’ve ever wondered whether there’s such a thing as budget CCW gear that’s actually usable, this video is for you. Drop a comment with your favorite hidden Amazon gun gear finds, let me know what I missed, and if this video does well maybe I’ll do a round two with even more weird budget gear. Big thanks to Osight for sponsoring part of this video and sending over the Osight SE for comparison.

title: Vivo X200T Camera Hack! Make Your Own ND Filter Case for Cinematic Video
description:
Turn your Vivo X200T into a powerful filmmaking tool with this simple DIY hack! 🎬

In this video, I show you how to convert a regular Vivo X200T phone case into an ND filter holding case, making it super easy to capture professional cinematic videos right from your smartphone.

ND filters help control light, reduce overexposure, and give you that smooth motion blur used in movies — and now you can achieve the same look on your phone!
📌 What you’ll learn in this video:
• How to modify a Vivo X200T case step-by-step
• How to attach ND filters to your phone

💡 This is perfect for:
Mobile filmmakers | Content creators | Travel vloggers | Instagram reel creators

🎥 With this simple DIY accessory, you can seriously upgrade your mobile videography and shoot like a pro!
👍 If you found this helpful, don’t forget to:
Like 👍 | Share 🔄 | Subscribe 🔔
#VivoX200T #MobileFilmmaking #NDFilter #CinematicVideo #DIYCamera #SmartphoneVideography

For collaborations : travellkumar@gmail.com

text_original:
Great video, always love an EDC. I’ve been using a modded iPod and I agree it’s made listening so much more purposeful. For me I’m so easily distracted and using a purpose built device is such a joy to just LISTEN to music and not check socials etc. it’s actually led me to not using any social accounts anymore except for YouTube if that counts as one.

I’ve started using an iPad mini daily too, really love the form factor, great content viewer and handy for note taking and more complex tasks that would be difficult on a phone.

I’ve got a 60s Tudor Oyster Prince which I love but I’ve recently bought an Accurist classic for everyday wear that I really enjoy and don’t worry too much about it getting scuffed (or destroyed the by the dog/kids 😂 )

I’ve gone for the Panasonic lumix lx100 for a travel cam. It’s nice and compact-ish and get nice results. Sony is always great though and I do use them for work.

Thanks again for the great vids!!!🎉

In this video, I show you how to convert a regular Vivo X200T phone case into an ND filter holding case, making it super easy to capture professional cinematic videos right from your smartphone.

ND filters help control light, reduce overexposure, and give you that smooth motion blur used in movies — and now you can achieve the same look on your phone!

text_original:
Looks nice but I prefer a bag with an external water bottle holder. 
My Vanguard 36 has traveled the world with a removable padded inset that holds camera/s the odd lens, an umbrella a small waterproof and two small water bottles one for me and one for my wife. 
On planes I take the insert with high value gear in a small Eastpak 24L backpack and the Vanguard goes flat in my suitcase. Then when I’m on tour the Vanguard is used every day and the backpack is flat in the suitcase.
Eastpak make great small backpacks that come with 30yr guarantees and teach you to travel light and anonymous.

title: The Best Budget Camera Sling for Travel Photography
description:
In this video, I test the K&F Concept Alpha 10L camera sling to see if downsizing your setup actually improves your travel photography experience. Instead of packing everything “just in case”, I limit myself to what fits inside this small sling and put it through a full day of shooting.

I break down what you can realistically fit inside, from simple camera setups to drone and vlogging options, and show the trade-offs you’ll have to make along the way. More importantly, I take it out into real travel conditions to see how it actually performs when you’re walking, shooting, and dealing with the heat.

If you’re tired of overpacking and want a lighter, simpler setup for travel photography, this might change how you think about your gear.

text_original:
A little long... sorry in advance...

I've enjoyed following your journey. I love how serious you take photography. I consider photography my passion... so I relate. I'm just a dad but I am my family's documentarian (something I take very seriously).  I've only recently gotten into film photography... July 2025. I too shot fujifilm (still haven't sold anything) for 10 years which led me to Leica. In 2023, I added the M11P which I feel led me to the MP. Since picking up the MP, I really haven't shot the M11 much. From the jump, I immersed myself in the end-to-end workflow or film (developing/scanning). For focal lengths, I say that I see the world in 35mm, but my heart feels the world in 28mm... 28mm also lends itself well to capturing a family of four easily without taking steps back. I visited 6 countries in 2025 with my family and the 28mm accounted for about 80% of my shots. For the Leica system, I currently have the thypoch simeras in 28/35/50 and I have the voigtlander color skopars in the same focal lengths. I recently purchased the Mandler 35mm to try (it will come in next week). When traveling, I have only been taking the simeras for the f/1.4. Like you, I am planning on getting the 28mm Summicron and I will also add the 35mm Summilux... they are my two most used focal lengths, so I want to have the Leica equivalents. I'll most likely also add the 50mm Summilux at some point.

What I love about my push into film photography... for the past several years, I've become more obsessed with the concept of 'planned obsolescence.' IMO, film cameras are the antidote to photography-focused planned obsolescence.I love the history, quality and mythology behind the Leica brand. I feel their commitment to photography is unrivaled. I also am a little romantic.. I want to be able to pass my leica equipment to my two young sons... so this week, I added another MP (black paint to join my silver MP)... one for each of the boys one day. I love how these cameras will outlive me and even if they never use them... they will hopefully think of me every time they see/pick them up. 


Congrats on the Summicron.

title: The Best Budget Camera Sling for Travel Photography
description:
In this video, I test the K&F Concept Alpha 10L camera sling to see if downsizing your setup actually improves your travel photography experience. Instead of packing everything “just in case”, I limit myself to what fits inside this small sling and put it through a full day of shooting.

I break down what you can realistically fit inside, from simple camera setups to drone and vlogging options, and show the trade-offs you’ll have to make along the way. More importantly, I take it out into real travel conditions to see how it actually performs when you’re walking, shooting, and dealing with the heat.

If you’re tired of overpacking and want a lighter, simpler setup for travel photography, this might change how you think about your gear.

title: The Best Budget Camera Sling for Travel Photography
description:
In this video, I test the K&F Concept Alpha 10L camera sling to see if downsizing your setup actually improves your travel photography experience. Instead of packing everything “just in case”, I limit myself to what fits inside this small sling and put it through a full day of shooting.

I break down what you can realistically fit inside, from simple camera setups to drone and vlogging options, and show the trade-offs you’ll have to make along the way. More importantly, I take it out into real travel conditions to see how it actually performs when you’re walking, shooting, and dealing with the heat.

If you’re tired of overpacking and want a lighter, simpler setup for travel photography, this might change how you think about your gear.

title: Vivo X200T Camera Hack! Make Your Own ND Filter Case for Cinematic Video
description:
Turn your Vivo X200T into a powerful filmmaking tool with this simple DIY hack! 🎬

In this video, I show you how to convert a regular Vivo X200T phone case into an ND filter holding case, making it super easy to capture professional cinematic videos right from your smartphone.

ND filters help control light, reduce overexposure, and give you that smooth motion blur used in movies — and now you can achieve the same look on your phone!
📌 What you’ll learn in this video:
• How to modify a Vivo X200T case step-by-step
• How to attach ND filters to your phone

💡 This is perfect for:
Mobile filmmakers | Content creators | Travel vloggers | Instagram reel creators

🎥 With this simple DIY accessory, you can seriously upgrade your mobile videography and shoot like a pro!
👍 If you found this helpful, don’t forget to:
Like 👍 | Share 🔄 | Subscribe 🔔

title: The Best Budget Camera Sling for Travel Photography
description:
In this video, I test the K&F Concept Alpha 10L camera sling to see if downsizing your setup actually improves your travel photography experience. Instead of packing everything “just in case”, I limit myself to what fits inside this small sling and put it through a full day of shooting.

I break down what you can realistically fit inside, from simple camera setups to drone and vlogging options, and show the trade-offs you’ll have to make along the way. More importantly, I take it out into real travel conditions to see how it actually performs when you’re walking, shooting, and dealing with the heat.

If you’re tired of overpacking and want a lighter, simpler setup for travel photography, this might change how you think about your gear.

For more travel and street photography content, make sure to subscribe.

text_original:
1 tip for the tech bag, or rather the cables.  If you have access to a 3d printer or a friend with one, there are a ton of cable winders out there.  I have my favorite, but my recommendation is to print different colors for different cables.  I also try to buy my cables that way too, so, USB-C is blue, Lightning is red, micro-usb is grey, etc.  That way, even if your cables aren't the right color, the 3d printer cable winders are and you can quickly/easily identify them.

Also, I've been looking for a tech bag, thank you!!

title: Vivo X200T Camera Hack! Make Your Own ND Filter Case for Cinematic Video
description:
Turn your Vivo X200T into a powerful filmmaking tool with this simple DIY hack! 🎬

In this video, I show you how to convert a regular Vivo X200T phone case into an ND filter holding case, making it super easy to capture professional cinematic videos right from your smartphone.

ND filters help control light, reduce overexposure, and give you that smooth motion blur used in movies — and now you can achieve the same look on your phone!
📌 What you’ll learn in this video:
• How to modify a Vivo X200T case step-by-step
• How to attach ND filters to your phone

💡 This is perfect for:
Mobile filmmakers | Content creators | Travel vloggers | Instagram reel creators

🎥 With this simple DIY accessory, you can seriously upgrade your mobile videography and shoot like a pro!

text_original: I love Jason's real world evals. I am surprised at the Ricoh Gr IV's  ISO range 100 - ~200,000, but I am curious to see how of much of this is actually usable. On my Sony A6700 once you get near the upper end of the ISO range, the sensor noise makes photos pretty much unusable. For me, the Sony RX100-7 is the cat's meow with it's 24-200mm. Personally, I will be waiting with bated breathe for the RX100-8 whenever that will appear as I don't know how long my RX100-7 will be working, as I have used the heck out of it and the black finish is starting to wear off at the edges, and as the old TimeX watch commercial goes, "I takes a licking and keeps on ticking." My previous pocket cameras with a self- extending/retracting zoom lens, a Canon S100 and Panasonic ZS50, would have long "give up to the ghost" at this point. ETA true the Sony RX100-7 is on the upper end of the price range, but I find it is truly 100% reliable, and if you format the SD-card after every photo/video session, you will always be GTG, as it has never failed me when I need it.

title: Samsung S26 Ultra - Best Camera Settings for Video and Photos!
description:
This is a practical, in-depth tutorial for the Samsung Galaxy S26 Ultra powered by the Snapdragon 8 Elite Gen 5 Mobile Platform for Galaxy.
We'll start with the essential photo settings and composition tools, then move into more advanced controls like autofocus tracking, resolution choices, and how the Qualcomm Spectra ISP + Neural Processing Unit work together for real-time scene optimization.
You’ll also see how the new Virtual Reflector works, which framerate to choose for video, and a full breakdown of APV LOG, including how it differs from HEVC and when to use each format.

Gear I Used in This video:
Freewell x StevenDivish Anamorphic Lens - http://bit.ly/4hgBHu7 
S26 Ultra Kevlar Case - https://bit.ly/3N5JOPd

Finally, I show two workflows for editing APV LOG footage: one directly on the S26 Ultra, and one on a computer for full professional control.

PART 1 (Photography Settings)
00:55 - Photo Settings for S26 Ultra Explained

03:35 - Camera Assistant (Lenses, Focus, Resolution)
05:34 - Advanced Photo Controls on S26 Ultra Explained
07:25 - Scanning and Processing Settings (SNAPDRAGON on-device processing)
09:00 - What resolution to use for photos on S26 Ultra?

11:04 - Editing with SPen on S26 Ultra
PART 2 (Video Settings)
11:52 - Video Settings
12:05 - Video Resolution HD or 4k Explained
12:30 - Video Framerate (30FPS or 60 FPS)
12:58 - Video Format (Bitrate, HDR, LOG) on S26 Ultra
14:19 - Samsung LUTS for S26 Ultra Videos
15:01 - APV Videos Explained
16:30 - APV Video Settings (LQ vs HQ vs HEVC)
17:50 - Should you choose LOG or HDR for videos?
18:52 - APV Editing (2 ways: on a phone or a laptop)

Make sure to subscribe and like this video it took a lot of time and effort to make this episode. Thank you for your support!

title: You Don’t Need a Better Camera - You Need a Fun One
description:
Fujifilm X100F Review (2026): Why This “Old” Camera Is Still the Best Everyday Camera 

The Fujifilm X100F might be nearly 10 years old—but it’s still the best everyday camera I own. Not because of specs… but because it actually makes you WANT to shoot.

In this video, I break down why the best camera isn’t always the most powerful—it’s the one you’ll carry every day. The Fujifilm X100F stands out because it’s compact, fun, and effortless to use, making it perfect for capturing real-life moments you’d otherwise miss.

You’ll hear real stories from everyday situations—like a simple walk to work turning into a meaningful photography moment, or spontaneous city shots that help build confidence behind the lens. This isn’t about megapixels or specs—it’s about consistency, experience, and growth as a photographer.

Key takeaways:

Why ease of use beats specs for daily photography
How carrying a camera daily improves your skills faster
Real-life moments you’d miss without a dedicated camera
The importance of “getting reps in” as a photographer
Why meaningful photos are better than perfect photos

Timestamps:
0:00 – Why the Fujifilm X100F is my best camera
0:45 – It’s not about specs or image quality
1:30 – The power of an everyday carry camera
2:30 – Story #1: Walk to work & unexpected interaction
5:30 – Why these moments matter for growth
6:15 – Downtown photo walk before dinner
7:15 – Street photography discomfort & lessons
8:30 – Getting reps in = getting better
9:30 – Capturing meaningful family moments
11:00 – Sharing the camera with others
12:15 – Final thoughts: Any camera can do this

If you enjoyed this video, make sure to  like, drop a comment with your go-to everyday camera, and subscribe for more real-world photography content.

#FujifilmX100F #EverydayCarry #StreetPhotography #PhotographyTips #DigitalPhotography

title: You Don’t Need a Better Camera - You Need a Fun One
description:
Fujifilm X100F Review (2026): Why This “Old” Camera Is Still the Best Everyday Camera 

The Fujifilm X100F might be nearly 10 years old—but it’s still the best everyday camera I own. Not because of specs… but because it actually makes you WANT to shoot.

In this video, I break down why the best camera isn’t always the most powerful—it’s the one you’ll carry every day. The Fujifilm X100F stands out because it’s compact, fun, and effortless to use, making it perfect for capturing real-life moments you’d otherwise miss.

You’ll hear real stories from everyday situations—like a simple walk to work turning into a meaningful photography moment, or spontaneous city shots that help build confidence behind the lens. This isn’t about megapixels or specs—it’s about consistency, experience, and growth as a photographer.

Key takeaways:

Why ease of use beats specs for daily photography
How carrying a camera daily improves your skills faster
Real-life moments you’d miss without a dedicated camera
The importance of “getting reps in” as a photographer
Why meaningful photos are better than perfect photos

Timestamps:
0:00 – Why the Fujifilm X100F is my best camera
0:45 – It’s not about specs or image quality
1:30 – The power of an everyday carry camera
2:30 – Story #1: Walk to work & unexpected interaction
5:30 – Why these moments matter for growth
6:15 – Downtown photo walk before dinner
7:15 – Street photography discomfort & lessons
8:30 – Getting reps in = getting better
9:30 – Capturing meaningful family moments
11:00 – Sharing the camera with others
12:15 – Final thoughts: Any camera can do this

If you enjoyed this video, make sure to  like, drop a comment with your go-to everyday camera, and subscribe for more real-world photography content.

#FujifilmX100F #EverydayCarry #StreetPhotography #PhotographyTips #DigitalPhotography

title: The Best Budget Camera Sling for Travel Photography
description:
In this video, I test the K&F Concept Alpha 10L camera sling to see if downsizing your setup actually improves your travel photography experience. Instead of packing everything “just in case”, I limit myself to what fits inside this small sling and put it through a full day of shooting.

I break down what you can realistically fit inside, from simple camera setups to drone and vlogging options, and show the trade-offs you’ll have to make along the way. More importantly, I take it out into real travel conditions to see how it actually performs when you’re walking, shooting, and dealing with the heat.

If you’re tired of overpacking and want a lighter, simpler setup for travel photography, this might change how you think about your gear.

title: This Lens Could Replace Your Entire Camera Bag / The Sigma 16-300mm Super Zoom Lens
description:
So I finally got my chance to use the Sigma 16-300 mm APS-C super zoom lens on my trip to Roatan and it is so good. This lens literally replaced my entire camera bag, in fact I didn’t even bring my camera bag, I replaced that with just a small camera sling my Sony a6700 and this lens. I’ve never used a lens with such huge focal range, which literally replaced all my lenses. Paired with a great built in stabilization, this APS-C lens does it all and is the ultimate travel lens!

title: Samsung S26 Ultra - Best Camera Settings for Video and Photos!
description:
This is a practical, in-depth tutorial for the Samsung Galaxy S26 Ultra powered by the Snapdragon 8 Elite Gen 5 Mobile Platform for Galaxy.
We'll start with the essential photo settings and composition tools, then move into more advanced controls like autofocus tracking, resolution choices, and how the Qualcomm Spectra ISP + Neural Processing Unit work together for real-time scene optimization.
You’ll also see how the new Virtual Reflector works, which framerate to choose for video, and a full breakdown of APV LOG, including how it differs from HEVC and when to use each format.

Gear I Used in This video:
Freewell x StevenDivish Anamorphic Lens - http://bit.ly/4hgBHu7 
S26 Ultra Kevlar Case - https://bit.ly/3N5JOPd

Finally, I show two workflows for editing APV LOG footage: one directly on the S26 Ultra, and one on a computer for full professional control.

Timecodes:
00:00 - Shot on S26 Ultra
PART 1 (Photography Settings)
00:55 - Photo Settings for S26 Ultra Explained
01:35 - Composition Guide Setting
02:07 - Autofocus and Filters (SNAPDRAGON Qualcomm Spectra ISP and Qualcomm Hexagon NPU Neural Processing)
03:35 - Camera Assistant (Lenses, Focus, Resolution)
05:34 - Advanced Photo Controls on S26 Ultra Explained
07:25 - Scanning and Processing Settings (SNAPDRAGON on-device processing)
09:00 - What resolution to use for photos on S26 Ultra?
10:07 - Virtual Reflector on S26 Ultra Tutorial (SNAPDRAGON real-time processing)
11:04 - Editing with SPen on S26 Ultra
PART 2 (Video Settings)
11:52 - Video Settings
12:05 - Video Resolution HD or 4k Explained
12:30 - Video Framerate (30FPS or 60 FPS)
12:58 - Video Format (Bitrate, HDR, LOG) on S26 Ultra
14:19 - Samsung LUTS for S26 Ultra Videos
15:01 - APV Videos Explained
16:30 - APV Video Settings (LQ vs HQ vs HEVC)
17:50 - Should you choose LOG or HDR for videos?
18:52 - APV Editing (2 ways: on a phone or a laptop)

Make sure to subscribe and like this video it took a lot of time and effort to make this episode. Thank you for your support!

#SponsoredBySnapdragon #s26ultra

description:
If you’ve been searching for the perfect everyday carry camera, this might help you rethink everything.

In this video, I share why I sold my Fujifilm X100V, Fujifilm XF10, and Sony ZV-1—and how I ended up choosing the Canon G7X Mark II as my daily go-to.

This isn’t a spec-heavy breakdown. It’s about real-world use, creativity, and finding a camera you actually want to carry and shoot with every day.

Here’s what I cover:
– Why the Fuji X100V stopped working for my workflow

title: The Best Budget Camera Sling for Travel Photography

In this video, I test the K&F Concept Alpha 10L camera sling to see if downsizing your setup actually improves your travel photography experience. Instead of packing everything “just in case”, I limit myself to what fits inside this small sling and put it through a full day of shooting.

I break down what you can realistically fit inside, from simple camera setups to drone and vlogging options, and show the trade-offs you’ll have to make along the way. More importantly, I take it out into real travel conditions to see how it actually performs when you’re walking, shooting, and dealing with the heat.

We'll start with the essential photo settings and composition tools, then move into more advanced controls like autofocus tracking, resolution choices, and how the Qualcomm Spectra ISP + Neural Processing Unit work together for real-time scene optimization.
You’ll also see how the new Virtual Reflector works, which framerate to choose for video, and a full breakdown of APV LOG, including how it differs from HEVC and when to use each format.

title: You Don’t Need a Better Camera - You Need a Fun One
description:
Fujifilm X100F Review (2026): Why This “Old” Camera Is Still the Best Everyday Camera 

The Fujifilm X100F might be nearly 10 years old—but it’s still the best everyday camera I own. Not because of specs… but because it actually makes you WANT to shoot.

In this video, I break down why the best camera isn’t always the most powerful—it’s the one you’ll carry every day. The Fujifilm X100F stands out because it’s compact, fun, and effortless to use, making it perfect for capturing real-life moments you’d otherwise miss.

You’ll hear real stories from everyday situations—like a simple walk to work turning into a meaningful photography moment, or spontaneous city shots that help build confidence behind the lens. This isn’t about megapixels or specs—it’s about consistency, experience, and growth as a photographer.

Key takeaways:

Why ease of use beats specs for daily photography
How carrying a camera daily improves your skills faster
Real-life moments you’d miss without a dedicated camera
The importance of “getting reps in” as a photographer
Why meaningful photos are better than perfect photos

Timestamps:
0:00 – Why the Fujifilm X100F is my best camera
0:45 – It’s not about specs or image quality
1:30 – The power of an everyday carry camera
2:30 – Story #1: Walk to work & unexpected interaction
5:30 – Why these moments matter for growth
6:15 – Downtown photo walk before dinner
7:15 – Street photography discomfort & lessons
8:30 – Getting reps in = getting better
9:30 – Capturing meaningful family moments
11:00 – Sharing the camera with others
12:15 – Final thoughts: Any camera can do this

If you enjoyed this video, make sure to  like, drop a comment with your go-to everyday camera, and subscribe for more real-world photography content.

#FujifilmX100F #EverydayCarry #StreetPhotography #PhotographyTips #DigitalPhotography

title: Samsung S26 Ultra - Best Camera Settings for Video and Photos!
description:

We'll start with the essential photo settings and composition tools, then move into more advanced controls like autofocus tracking, resolution choices, and how the Qualcomm Spectra ISP + Neural Processing Unit work together for real-time scene optimization.
You’ll also see how the new Virtual Reflector works, which framerate to choose for video, and a full breakdown of APV LOG, including how it differs from HEVC and when to use each format.

title: You Don’t Need a Better Camera - You Need a Fun One
description:
Fujifilm X100F Review (2026): Why This “Old” Camera Is Still the Best Everyday Camera 

The Fujifilm X100F might be nearly 10 years old—but it’s still the best everyday camera I own. Not because of specs… but because it actually makes you WANT to shoot.

In this video, I break down why the best camera isn’t always the most powerful—it’s the one you’ll carry every day. The Fujifilm X100F stands out because it’s compact, fun, and effortless to use, making it perfect for capturing real-life moments you’d otherwise miss.

You’ll hear real stories from everyday situations—like a simple walk to work turning into a meaningful photography moment, or spontaneous city shots that help build confidence behind the lens. This isn’t about megapixels or specs—it’s about consistency, experience, and growth as a photographer.

Key takeaways:

Why ease of use beats specs for daily photography
How carrying a camera daily improves your skills faster
Real-life moments you’d miss without a dedicated camera
The importance of “getting reps in” as a photographer
Why meaningful photos are better than perfect photos

Timestamps:
0:00 – Why the Fujifilm X100F is my best camera
0:45 – It’s not about specs or image quality
1:30 – The power of an everyday carry camera
2:30 – Story #1: Walk to work & unexpected interaction
5:30 – Why these moments matter for growth
6:15 – Downtown photo walk before dinner
7:15 – Street photography discomfort & lessons
8:30 – Getting reps in = getting better
9:30 – Capturing meaningful family moments
11:00 – Sharing the camera with others
12:15 – Final thoughts: Any camera can do this

If you enjoyed this video, make sure to  like, drop a comment with your go-to everyday camera, and subscribe for more real-world photography content.

#FujifilmX100F #EverydayCarry #StreetPhotography #PhotographyTips #DigitalPhotography

title: You Don’t Need a Better Camera - You Need a Fun One
description:
Fujifilm X100F Review (2026): Why This “Old” Camera Is Still the Best Everyday Camera 

The Fujifilm X100F might be nearly 10 years old—but it’s still the best everyday camera I own. Not because of specs… but because it actually makes you WANT to shoot.

In this video, I break down why the best camera isn’t always the most powerful—it’s the one you’ll carry every day. The Fujifilm X100F stands out because it’s compact, fun, and effortless to use, making it perfect for capturing real-life moments you’d otherwise miss.

You’ll hear real stories from everyday situations—like a simple walk to work turning into a meaningful photography moment, or spontaneous city shots that help build confidence behind the lens. This isn’t about megapixels or specs—it’s about consistency, experience, and growth as a photographer.

Key takeaways:

Why ease of use beats specs for daily photography
How carrying a camera daily improves your skills faster
Real-life moments you’d miss without a dedicated camera
The importance of “getting reps in” as a photographer
Why meaningful photos are better than perfect photos

Timestamps:
0:00 – Why the Fujifilm X100F is my best camera
0:45 – It’s not about specs or image quality
1:30 – The power of an everyday carry camera
2:30 – Story #1: Walk to work & unexpected interaction
5:30 – Why these moments matter for growth
6:15 – Downtown photo walk before dinner
7:15 – Street photography discomfort & lessons
8:30 – Getting reps in = getting better
9:30 – Capturing meaningful family moments
11:00 – Sharing the camera with others
12:15 – Final thoughts: Any camera can do this

If you enjoyed this video, make sure to  like, drop a comment with your go-to everyday camera, and subscribe for more real-world photography content.

#FujifilmX100F #EverydayCarry #StreetPhotography #PhotographyTips #DigitalPhotography

text_original: Congratulations on your first trip to Paris.  Planning my 7th to Paris and 10th to Europe.  In my opinion, that bag is too big.  Stay away from crowds and being packed into the Metro.  You will be a target as you are squeezed into a crowd and the pockets opened.  I am bringing a 9L sling with two mirrorless bodies as the daily carry and a larger than your bag for the lenses and other stuff.  Each day will be two bodies and maybe three lenses.  Some zooms and some primes, new and vintage.  Do you have target images in mind?  Specific locations you are targeting?  This is a graduation trip for our granddaughter and her first trip.  5 nights in London and 8 in Paris.  Some day trips via rental car from each city so we can experience Stone Henge and Giverny with her.  My first trip to Paris on 2005 was with film and a point and shoot.  Caught the bug and have really enjoyed photography.  Let me know if you have any questions.  We were last there in 2022 and 2021.

NO DECISION MAKING INVOLVED, IF ITS FRIDAY, I GRAB MY FRIDAY BAG.

Tuesday) Canon F1n w/ Canon FD 24mm f2.8 /red filter/ Tri-X / Black Domke f3x bag
Wednesday) Fujica GW690 w/90mm f3.5 Fujinon EBC/ Kodak Gold 120 / Domke Brown F3x ruggedwear
Thursday) Canon VTdeluxe Black body w/ Voigtlander 21mm f4 Color Skopar ltm / Orange filter / Rollei RPX 400 / ONA Prince Street Dark Truffle bag
Friday) Nikomat FTn  Black body w/ Nikkor 24mm f2.8 / yellow filter / Rollei RPX 100 / Domke F803  ruggedwear bag
Saturday) Canon VTdeluxe w/Voigtlander 21mm f4 Color Skopar ltm / yellow filter/ Tri-X / ONA Prince Street Dark Truffle bag
Sunday) Linhof 617s Technorama w/90mm Schneider Kreuznach f5.6 Super Angulon / Yellow filter / Tri-X pushed to 1600/ Eddie Bauer leather bag

text_original: Greetings. Disclaimer: on YouTubes these days, I'm an old timer; I've had cameras in my life since the late 1970s when we had no choice; our world was analogue. I've scanned my negatives from those days; they're all in great shape. Ok that's out of the way. I'm a little curious about why you feel you have to take a light meter along. When I was a young photographer (but art student in a different medium), I only used Kodak plusX now discontinued; it was ISO125 so let's say IlfordFP4 equivalent. I never worried about half the settings and craziness people worry about today and most of them have a computer in their hands. I bought a 1987 LeicaM6 about two years ago; the meter is extremely accurate. I've taken pictures indoors with difficult light with it using Delta400 or even FP4,  probably wide open at a 60th (I'm guessing) and the negatives have surprised me. Most of the Ilford B&W films have incredible exposure latitude. Even if my meter says I don't have enough light, I'll take it anyway. I've used simple mechanical cameras for a long time so I'm very comfortable with them. After a while you know what your camera and film stock will do. I have a couple digital cameras but I reach for the simple mechanical ones before I walk out the door. (I develop & scan at home; print with archival ink on rag or bamboo paper.)  Not sure how I can send you examples of some pictures I took in low light with the M6.

description:
A full unboxing of the DJI Osmo Pocket 4 Creator Combo is now out, and this looks like the final retail unit. The video reveals everything inside the box, including the camera, Battery Handle, Mini Tripod, Mic transmitter, fill light, and more.

text_original:
I would absolutely drop the spare knife for a spare mag, two mags makes a lot more sense and serves a lot more purpose than two sharp edges… your knife is a last ditch anyway… why not double the capacity of your primary weapon and just have one last ditch tool? You’re just adding redundancy to your last ditch tool over your primary weapon.

Add some medical. At minimum a Snakestaff ETQ 2.0 and consider some QuikClot/compact NAR chest seals. 

With this setup, you lack the quickest way to fix a malfunction, and you don’t have a way to keep yourself from bleeding out/dying from a sucking chest wound if you get clipped in the fight. Basically,  you’re SOL if you experience a malf or get hit in the gunfight. Bad spot to be. 

Overall  5/10

title: You Don’t Need a Better Camera - You Need a Fun One
description:
Fujifilm X100F Review (2026): Why This “Old” Camera Is Still the Best Everyday Camera 

The Fujifilm X100F might be nearly 10 years old—but it’s still the best everyday camera I own. Not because of specs… but because it actually makes you WANT to shoot.

In this video, I break down why the best camera isn’t always the most powerful—it’s the one you’ll carry every day. The Fujifilm X100F stands out because it’s compact, fun, and effortless to use, making it perfect for capturing real-life moments you’d otherwise miss.

You’ll hear real stories from everyday situations—like a simple walk to work turning into a meaningful photography moment, or spontaneous city shots that help build confidence behind the lens. This isn’t about megapixels or specs—it’s about consistency, experience, and growth as a photographer.

Key takeaways:

Why ease of use beats specs for daily photography
How carrying a camera daily improves your skills faster
Real-life moments you’d miss without a dedicated camera
The importance of “getting reps in” as a photographer
Why meaningful photos are better than perfect photos

title: You Don’t Need a Better Camera - You Need a Fun One
description:
Fujifilm X100F Review (2026): Why This “Old” Camera Is Still the Best Everyday Camera 

The Fujifilm X100F might be nearly 10 years old—but it’s still the best everyday camera I own. Not because of specs… but because it actually makes you WANT to shoot.

In this video, I break down why the best camera isn’t always the most powerful—it’s the one you’ll carry every day. The Fujifilm X100F stands out because it’s compact, fun, and effortless to use, making it perfect for capturing real-life moments you’d otherwise miss.

You’ll hear real stories from everyday situations—like a simple walk to work turning into a meaningful photography moment, or spontaneous city shots that help build confidence behind the lens. This isn’t about megapixels or specs—it’s about consistency, experience, and growth as a photographer.

Key takeaways:

Why ease of use beats specs for daily photography
How carrying a camera daily improves your skills faster
Real-life moments you’d miss without a dedicated camera
The importance of “getting reps in” as a photographer
Why meaningful photos are better than perfect photos

Timestamps:

title: Samsung S26 Ultra - Best Camera Settings for Video and Photos!
description:
This is a practical, in-depth tutorial for the Samsung Galaxy S26 Ultra powered by the Snapdragon 8 Elite Gen 5 Mobile Platform for Galaxy.
We'll start with the essential photo settings and composition tools, then move into more advanced controls like autofocus tracking, resolution choices, and how the Qualcomm Spectra ISP + Neural Processing Unit work together for real-time scene optimization.
You’ll also see how the new Virtual Reflector works, which framerate to choose for video, and a full breakdown of APV LOG, including how it differs from HEVC and when to use each format.

Gear I Used in This video:
Freewell x StevenDivish Anamorphic Lens - http://bit.ly/4hgBHu7 
S26 Ultra Kevlar Case - https://bit.ly/3N5JOPd

Finally, I show two workflows for editing APV LOG footage: one directly on the S26 Ultra, and one on a computer for full professional control.

02:07 - Autofocus and Filters (SNAPDRAGON Qualcomm Spectra ISP and Qualcomm Hexagon NPU Neural Processing)

title: You Don’t Need a Better Camera - You Need a Fun One
description:
Fujifilm X100F Review (2026): Why This “Old” Camera Is Still the Best Everyday Camera 

The Fujifilm X100F might be nearly 10 years old—but it’s still the best everyday camera I own. Not because of specs… but because it actually makes you WANT to shoot.

In this video, I break down why the best camera isn’t always the most powerful—it’s the one you’ll carry every day. The Fujifilm X100F stands out because it’s compact, fun, and effortless to use, making it perfect for capturing real-life moments you’d otherwise miss.

You’ll hear real stories from everyday situations—like a simple walk to work turning into a meaningful photography moment, or spontaneous city shots that help build confidence behind the lens. This isn’t about megapixels or specs—it’s about consistency, experience, and growth as a photographer.

Key takeaways:

Why ease of use beats specs for daily photography
How carrying a camera daily improves your skills faster
Real-life moments you’d miss without a dedicated camera
The importance of “getting reps in” as a photographer
Why meaningful photos are better than perfect photos

title: DJI Pocket 4 Unboxed — Creator Combo Fully Revealed.
description:
A full unboxing of the DJI Osmo Pocket 4 Creator Combo is now out, and this looks like the final retail unit. The video reveals everything inside the box, including the camera, Battery Handle, Mini Tripod, Mic transmitter, fill light, and more.

Design-wise, it stays close to the Pocket 3 but adds subtle refinements like a more compact case and extra buttons. Early details also hint at around 107GB internal storage and up to 14 stops of dynamic range.

There’s even an early pricing signal showing up from China, giving us a rough idea of where this might land ahead of launch.

text_original: My everyday carry camera is the OM System TG-7. Very robust and watertight down to 15 meters. Very important if like me living in a place where it rains quite often. Fits easily in a pocket, and the picture quality is pretty decent considering the tiny sensor. OK range on the lens, about 25 to 100mm in full frame terms. I even use it from time to time for dedicated photo-walks. It is way more discrete than my Canon R7 or R100, so people on the streets may just think of me as another tourist. With the R7 or R100, I am instantly pegged as a photographer, and some will actively avoid getting in the frame - or commit the cardinal sin, stare right into the lens.

title: You Don’t Need a Better Camera - You Need a Fun One
description:
Fujifilm X100F Review (2026): Why This “Old” Camera Is Still the Best Everyday Camera 

The Fujifilm X100F might be nearly 10 years old—but it’s still the best everyday camera I own. Not because of specs… but because it actually makes you WANT to shoot.

In this video, I break down why the best camera isn’t always the most powerful—it’s the one you’ll carry every day. The Fujifilm X100F stands out because it’s compact, fun, and effortless to use, making it perfect for capturing real-life moments you’d otherwise miss.

You’ll hear real stories from everyday situations—like a simple walk to work turning into a meaningful photography moment, or spontaneous city shots that help build confidence behind the lens. This isn’t about megapixels or specs—it’s about consistency, experience, and growth as a photographer.

Key takeaways:

Why ease of use beats specs for daily photography
How carrying a camera daily improves your skills faster
Real-life moments you’d miss without a dedicated camera
The importance of “getting reps in” as a photographer
Why meaningful photos are better than perfect photos

title: Samsung S26 Ultra - Best Camera Settings for Video and Photos!
description:
This is a practical, in-depth tutorial for the Samsung Galaxy S26 Ultra powered by the Snapdragon 8 Elite Gen 5 Mobile Platform for Galaxy.
We'll start with the essential photo settings and composition tools, then move into more advanced controls like autofocus tracking, resolution choices, and how the Qualcomm Spectra ISP + Neural Processing Unit work together for real-time scene optimization.
You’ll also see how the new Virtual Reflector works, which framerate to choose for video, and a full breakdown of APV LOG, including how it differs from HEVC and when to use each format.

Gear I Used in This video:
Freewell x StevenDivish Anamorphic Lens - http://bit.ly/4hgBHu7 
S26 Ultra Kevlar Case - https://bit.ly/3N5JOPd

Finally, I show two workflows for editing APV LOG footage: one directly on the S26 Ultra, and one on a computer for full professional control.

This is a practical, in-depth tutorial for the Samsung Galaxy S26 Ultra powered by the Snapdragon 8 Elite Gen 5 Mobile Platform for Galaxy.
We'll start with the essential photo settings and composition tools, then move into more advanced controls like autofocus tracking, resolution choices, and how the Qualcomm Spectra ISP + Neural Processing Unit work together for real-time scene optimization.
You’ll also see how the new Virtual Reflector works, which framerate to choose for video, and a full breakdown of APV LOG, including how it differs from HEVC and when to use each format.

Gear I Used in This video:
Freewell x StevenDivish Anamorphic Lens - http://bit.ly/4hgBHu7 
S26 Ultra Kevlar Case - https://bit.ly/3N5JOPd

Finally, I show two workflows for editing APV LOG footage: one directly on the S26 Ultra, and one on a computer for full professional control.

Timecodes:
00:00 - Shot on S26 Ultra
PART 1 (Photography Settings)
00:55 - Photo Settings for S26 Ultra Explained
01:35 - Composition Guide Setting
02:07 - Autofocus and Filters (SNAPDRAGON Qualcomm Spectra ISP and Qualcomm Hexagon NPU Neural Processing)
03:35 - Camera Assistant (Lenses, Focus, Resolution)
05:34 - Advanced Photo Controls on S26 Ultra Explained
07:25 - Scanning and Processing Settings (SNAPDRAGON on-device processing)
09:00 - What resolution to use for photos on S26 Ultra?
10:07 - Virtual Reflector on S26 Ultra Tutorial (SNAPDRAGON real-time processing)
11:04 - Editing with SPen on S26 Ultra
PART 2 (Video Settings)
11:52 - Video Settings
12:05 - Video Resolution HD or 4k Explained
12:30 - Video Framerate (30FPS or 60 FPS)
12:58 - Video Format (Bitrate, HDR, LOG) on S26 Ultra
14:19 - Samsung LUTS for S26 Ultra Videos
15:01 - APV Videos Explained
16:30 - APV Video Settings (LQ vs HQ vs HEVC)
17:50 - Should you choose LOG or HDR for videos?
18:52 - APV Editing (2 ways: on a phone or a laptop)

text_original:
Thanks for the review.  I was enjoying the Tamron 18-300mm as my main travel kit since 2021 but in 2025 I switched to the Sigma 16-300mm. The 16mm(24mm equivalent) makes a big improvement in my quest to find the best super zoom travel kit. Part of the reason to switch to the A6700 & Sigma 16-300 combo was the slightly lower weight and it’s very sharp at 16mm and is sharper at 300mm than the Tamron.
My “new” standard travel kit now comprises the A6700 together with the Sigma 16-300mm (1108g) and the Sony A6400 with the Sony 10-20mm (581g). This kit is relatively light at only 1,689g and provides a 15-450mm equivalent reach without having to switch lenses. I just recently used this setup for a trip to Andalusia, Spain. Depending upon what and where you want to photograph you can select either body or lens combination or you can also leave one body behind or even just take one lens and body together with your phone as I just did for a visit to Palermo, Sicily. Depending upon your shooting style and interests especially in “old city environments” and for landscapes you have an unbelievable flexibility in choosing a mix of both details and/or a wider perspective.

text_original: Sony has a 24-50 F2.8 and I wish we had that for Canon.  I use the 28-70 2.8 which is a great street and indoors lens.  If I know I'll be inside I'll also bring the 24mm 1.8 for the close and wide stuff, possibly on a second body.  Which bodies?  Doesn't matter for street as long as it is full frame (would need different lens choices for crop).  I have R6-3 and R8, either will do for street.  Without IBIS I would definitely use lenses with stabilisation for the R8 especially indoors or low light.  R5-2 is definitely overkill for street, if I had one I'd leave it at home.

text_original:
LOVE this lens for day time shooting (not night). I use f6.3 on my Sony a6700 So the aperture stays the same until 270mm.  Zoom ring is kind of stiff like you pointed out, its hard to make a clean consistent zoom without moving the camera a lot, so NOT great for videography zoomin. And the low light is NOT good. But this lens is awesome for daytime, and macro. Pair this with a cheap and lightweight  Viltrox 1.7, or Yongnuo 1.8, or even TTartisan 27mm 2.8 pancake for nighttime, and you will only need 2 lenses.

Also, nice video :)
